slides.title: Know Your Enemy
slides.subtitle: Cyber Foundations: the five types of adversaries — script kiddies, hacktivists, insiders, cyberterrorists, and transnational criminal organizations — and what motivates each
slides.footerLeft: AP Cybersecurity · College Board CED (Effective Fall 2026)
slides.script: Welcome back to Lesson 2.1, Cyber Foundations, Day 2 of 8. Yesterday, 2.1.A, you learned how social engineers manipulate a person. Today, 2.1.B, you learn who is on the other end of those attacks — the five types of adversaries the College Board names, and what drives each one. This matters because motivation predicts behavior: a script kiddie chasing recognition behaves nothing like a transnational criminal organization chasing profit, and an insider with a badge is a different kind of threat entirely. Know the adversary and you can anticipate the attack.

slides.prompt: Three breaches hit the news the same week. (1) A teenager defaces a school website using a tool he downloaded but can't really explain. (2) A departing employee copies the customer database on his last day. (3) A group locks a hospital's files and demands payment in cryptocurrency.
slides.subprompt: Write 3–5 sentences: for each breach, what do you think the attacker actually wanted? Are they all after the same thing? What makes the employee case different from the other two?
slides.directions: Independent writing. I will cold-call 2–3 students.
slides.script: Two minutes, on your own. Three breaches in one week: a teenager defaces a school site with a tool he can't explain; a departing employee copies the customer database on his way out; a group locks a hospital's files for ransom. In three to five sentences, tell me what each attacker actually wanted. Are they all after the same thing? And what makes the employee fundamentally different from the other two? Hold your answer — each of these is a named adversary type in today's standard, and the thing that separates them is motivation and access.

slides.heading: What You’ll Be Filling In
slides.subheading: Your Day 2 Guided Notes have two sections that match today’s lesson. Both are CB-required.

sections.title: Lower-Resource Adversaries
sections.description: Script kiddies, hacktivists, and insiders — defined by skill, cause, and access rather than by large organizations (EK 2.1.B.1–B.3).

sections.title: Organized and State-Level Adversaries
sections.description: Cyberterrorists and transnational criminal organizations — disruption of communities versus financial gain at scale (EK 2.1.B.4–B.5).

slides.note: Fill in your Guided Notes as we go. The Lesson 2.1 page on apcsexamprep.com has every answer if you fall behind.
slides.script: Two sections today, both CB-required. First, the lower-resource adversaries: script kiddies defined by low skill, hacktivists defined by a cause, and insiders defined by legitimate access. Second, the organized and state-level adversaries: cyberterrorists who target whole communities, and transnational criminal organizations chasing money at scale. For every type, the College Board gives you a motivation — that's the column to fill carefully, because the exam tests adversary by motivation. Keep the packet open.

slides.heading: By the End of Today You Will...

items.text: Name the five adversary types — script kiddie, hacktivist, insider, cyberterrorist, and transnational criminal organization — and the motivation that defines each (LO 2.1.B)
Match a breach description to the correct adversary type using its motive and target (LO 2.1.B)
Explain why an insider is a unique threat and why motivation and access — not just skill — determine how dangerous an adversary is (LO 2.1.B)

slides.script: Three things to leave with today, all under learning objective 2.1.B, identify types of adversaries. First, name the five types — script kiddie, hacktivist, insider, cyberterrorist, transnational criminal organization — and pin each to its defining motivation: recognition or greed, a cause, revenge with access, disruption of communities, or financial gain at scale. Second, take a breach description and match it to the right adversary using motive and target, the way the exam asks. Third, explain why the insider is called a unique threat, and why motivation and access — not raw skill — decide how dangerous an adversary really is. By the end of today you can read a breach and name the adversary.

slides.label: Lower-Resource Adversaries
slides.script: Section one. Learning objective 2.1.B. The first three adversary types — defined not by big budgets but by skill level, by a cause, and by access. Fifteen minutes here.

slides.heading: Three Adversaries: Skill, Cause, and Access
slides.subheading: EK 2.1.B.1, B.2, and B.3 — script kiddies, hacktivists, and insider adversaries.

items.title: Script Kiddies
items.summary: Low-skilled adversaries who use tools developed by others without understanding how the tools work. They are often motivated by greed or a desire for recognition.

items.title: Hacktivists
items.summary: Adversaries motivated by social, political, or personal causes. They compromise computers and networks to support their cause or stop perceived harm, believing their goals justify their illegal methods.

items.title: Insider Adversaries
items.summary: Unique threats because they have legitimate credentials and access to systems and data. They can be recruited by malicious third parties and can be motivated by greed or revenge.

slides.script: Three adversaries, each defined by one distinctive thing. EK 2.1.B.1, script kiddies: low-skilled, they run tools other people built without understanding how those tools work, and they're usually after greed or recognition — bragging rights. EK 2.1.B.2, hacktivists: motivated by a social, political, or personal cause, they compromise systems to support that cause or stop harm they perceive, and crucially they believe their goals justify breaking the law. EK 2.1.B.3, insider adversaries: the unique one, because they already have legitimate credentials and access — they don't have to break in. They can be recruited by outsiders and are often driven by greed or revenge. Write all three with EK numbers, and write the one word that defines each: skill, cause, access.

slides.label: Organized and State-Level Adversaries
slides.script: Section two. Still 2.1.B. The two highest-impact adversaries — one aiming to disrupt whole communities, the other to profit at scale. Twelve minutes.

slides.heading: Disruption vs. Profit at Scale
slides.subheading: EK 2.1.B.4 and B.5 — cyberterrorists target civil infrastructure; transnational criminal organizations target money.

left.title: Cyberterrorists (EK 2.1.B.4)
left.definition: Motivated by politics or beliefs; aim to disrupt communities
examples: Seek to disrupt entire communities, regions, or nations through cyberattacks
Targets include power grids, water treatment plants, and other civil infrastructure
Can act independently or on behalf of governments or criminal organizations

right.title: Transnational Criminal Organizations (EK 2.1.B.5)
right.definition: Motivated by financial gain at scale
examples: Seek financial gain primarily through cybercrime
Sell the stolen IP in illegal markets

slides.footer: AP TIP: Both are high-impact, but the MOTIVE separates them — cyberterrorists want disruption of communities and infrastructure (EK 2.1.B.4); transnational criminal organizations want money (EK 2.1.B.5). Match the target to the motive.
slides.script: Two columns, two motives. On the left, EK 2.1.B.4, cyberterrorists: driven by politics or beliefs, they want to disrupt entire communities, regions, or nations, which is why their targets are civil infrastructure — power grids, water treatment plants. And they can act on their own or on behalf of a government or criminal organization. On the right, EK 2.1.B.5, transnational criminal organizations: pure financial gain, primarily by deploying ransomware to extort victims and stealing corporate intellectual property to sell in illegal markets. Both are serious, but the tell is the motive — disruption of a community versus money. Match the target to the motive and you'll name the adversary every time. Write both EKs.

slides.heading: The ‘Skill Equals Danger’ Myth
slides.misconception: Only highly skilled attackers are dangerous; a low-skill script kiddie or a trusted employee can't do real damage.
slides.whyItHappens: We picture a hacker as a coding genius, so adversaries who don't write their own tools — or who are just ‘a coworker’ — feel harmless by comparison. Skill becomes a stand-in for threat level.
slides.correction: The CED defines adversaries by motivation and access, not just skill. Script kiddies (EK 2.1.B.1) can do real harm with tools others built, especially when well-documented exploits lower the skill needed. Insider adversaries (EK 2.1.B.3) are explicitly called unique threats — not for their skill, but because they already hold legitimate credentials and access. Danger comes from motive and access as much as from technical ability.
slides.example: An insider with valid login credentials can copy a customer database in minutes with no exploit at all — no firewall stops a legitimate login. Meanwhile a script kiddie running a downloaded ransomware kit can lock a small clinic's files without understanding a line of the code. Neither needed elite skill to cause serious damage.
slides.script: Here's the misconception, and the exam leans on it. We imagine a hacker as a genius coder, so the low-skill script kiddie and the ordinary employee seem harmless. But read the standard: the College Board defines these adversaries by motivation and access, not just skill. A script kiddie with a downloaded ransomware kit can lock a clinic's files cold — and remember from the risk lessons that well-documented exploits lower the skill bar so more people can pull them off. The insider is the sharpest example: EK 2.1.B.3 literally calls them a unique threat, not because they're skilled but because they already have legitimate credentials and access — no firewall stops a valid login. So when a scenario describes a ‘basic’ attacker or a trusted employee, don't dismiss them. Motive plus access is the threat.

slides.heading: Key Vocabulary (LO 2.1.B)

terms.definition: A low-skilled adversary who uses tools developed by others without understanding how they work, often motivated by greed or a desire for recognition (EK 2.1.B.1).
An adversary motivated by a social, political, or personal cause who compromises systems to support that cause, believing the goals justify illegal methods (EK 2.1.B.2).
A unique threat who already has legitimate credentials and access to systems and data, who can be recruited by third parties and motivated by greed or revenge (EK 2.1.B.3).
An adversary motivated by politics or beliefs who seeks to disrupt communities, regions, or nations by attacking civil infrastructure (EK 2.1.B.4).
An adversary seeking financial gain primarily by deploying ransomware and stealing corporate intellectual property to sell in illegal markets (EK 2.1.B.5).
Malicious software that locks or encrypts a victim's data to extort payment, the primary tool of transnational criminal organizations (EK 2.1.B.5).

slides.script: Six terms. The five adversary types are the core — script kiddie, hacktivist, insider adversary, cyberterrorist, transnational criminal organization — and for each, the motivation is what you memorize, because that's how the exam asks. The sixth, ransomware, is the signature tool of the transnational criminal organization, EK 2.1.B.5, and you'll meet it again in the attack-phases lesson. Get all six into your notes with EK numbers, and pair each adversary with its one-word motive.

slides.heading: Stop and Think — Independent Writing
slides.subheading: Both questions map to LO 2.1.B. Complete on your Guided Notes packet.
prompts: A nation's power grid is knocked offline by attackers who release a statement about a political grievance and claim no ransom is wanted. Name the most likely adversary type, cite its EK, and explain how the target and the stated motive point to that type.

Explain why the CED calls insider adversaries a ‘unique threat.’ Contrast an insider with an external script kiddie of equal technical skill: who is more dangerous to a company's confidential data, and why? Cite EK 2.1.B.1 and EK 2.1.B.3.
slides.directions: Independent. 6 minutes. Full sentences. I will collect Guided Notes at the end of class.
slides.script: Two questions, six minutes, independent, full sentences. Question one: a power grid goes down, the attackers cite a political grievance, no ransom — name the adversary, that's a cyberterrorist, EK 2.1.B.4, and explain how the infrastructure target and the political motive give it away. Question two is the heart of today: why does the CED call insiders a unique threat? Contrast an insider with an equally skilled outside script kiddie — who's more dangerous to confidential data and why? Cite EK 2.1.B.1 and B.3. The answer is access. Be precise.

The quiz at the bottom is five questions across the whole topic — keep it on your radar; it comes at the end of the lesson. Finish both exercises before tomorrow.

slides.heading: End of Day 2
todayPoints: Named the five adversary types and the motivation that defines each (LO 2.1.B)
Distinguished script kiddies by low skill, hacktivists by cause, insiders by legitimate access (EK 2.1.B.1–B.3)
Separated cyberterrorists (disrupt communities and infrastructure) from transnational criminal organizations (financial gain at scale) (EK 2.1.B.4–B.5)
Learned that motivation and access — not just skill — determine how dangerous an adversary is
nextPoints: Describe the six phases of a cyberattack, in order (LO 2.1.C)
Trace how an adversary moves from reconnaissance to taking action to evading detection
Keep the Lesson 2.1 quiz on the horizon — it spans all of 2.1.A through G
slides.teaser: Tonight, think about HOW one of these adversaries would actually break in. Where would they start? It's never with the attack itself — it starts with quietly gathering information. Tomorrow you'll learn the six phases every cyberattack can move through, in order.
slides.script: Day 2 done. You can name all five adversary types and, for each, the motivation that defines it — and you understand that motive plus access, not raw skill, is what makes an adversary dangerous. Tonight's teaser sets up tomorrow: now that you know who attacks, we look at how. Tomorrow, EK 2.1.C, the six phases of a cyberattack — from quietly gathering information all the way to covering their tracks. Review your Guided Notes — be able to name each adversary and its motivation cold.
